entries.value: Use the name prop to ensure there is an alternative text equivalent for screen readers.
To avoid confusion, do not use a tooltip with an avatar when it is non-interactive or disabled, as the tooltip may not work consistently for keyboard users and screen readers.
Never use an avatar in isolation without copy or interaction cues (such as a chevron icon). This is so that they are clearly identifiable, and aid comprehension and interaction.
Check you’re using the appropriately sized shape and sized avatar for your use case.
Body: The image representing the user or entity.
Status: An icon that displays the avatar’s status.
Presence: Indicates if a user is available, away, focused, or busy.
An avatar is a visual representation of a user or entity.
An avatar is a thumbnail representation of an entity, such as a user or an organization.
An avatar shows an image or text to represent a person or group as well as give additional information like their status and activity.
circular avatars to quickly identify users
square avatars to help identify large product entities like projects, spaces, groups, rooms, or repositories
a presence indicator to indicate presence for a single user
Use branded generic avatars when a user has not set their avatar image. These images are designed to be abstracted from all genders, locales, and cultures.
If no profile image is available fallback to initials on the text avatar type.
If no logo is available fallback to category icon on the icon type.
Don’t make assumptions and use gendered placeholder avatars.
Don’t add more than 2 characters.
Don’t use more than one badge in the avatar.
Avoid using a double horizontal avatar if it affects the content alignment, such as in a navigation option.
Don’t use more than 2 avatars.
